era_context: The early Han Dynasty under Emperor Wen is consolidating power. In the Mediterranean, the Roman Republic is in the midst of the Second Macedonian War (200–196 BCE) against Philip V of Macedon. The Seleucid Empire under Antiochus III is expanding aggressively. Egypt under Ptolemy V is weakening. The Fourth Syrian War (197–194 BCE) between the Seleucids and Ptolemies is ongoing or about to begin.

events.title: Second Macedonian War: Roman campaigns in Greece
events.description: The Roman general Titus Quinctius Flamininus continues military operations against Philip V of Macedon in the Second Macedonian War (200–196 BCE). In 198 BCE, Flamininus takes command of the Roman forces and begins a series of campaigns designed to defeat Philip and establish Roman dominance in Greece. The campaigns involve maneuvering for position, diplomatic negotiations with Greek city-states, and military engagements. Flamininus's strategy is to isolate Philip by winning over Greek allies and then defeating him in a decisive battle.
events.certainty_note: The Second Macedonian War is well-attested in multiple sources. The year 198 BCE marks Flamininus's assumption of command, which is clearly dated in the sources.

events.title: Antiochus III consolidates power in the Seleucid Empire
events.description: Antiochus III continues his policy of consolidating Seleucid power through military campaigns and administrative reforms. In 198 BCE, he is at the height of his power, controlling vast territories from Asia Minor to Bactria. He is preparing for military campaigns in Coele-Syria and is beginning to attract the attention of Rome. His consolidation of power is part of a broader strategy to make the Seleucid Empire the dominant power in the Eastern Mediterranean.
events.certainty_note: Antiochus III's consolidation of power occurred over several years (200–192 BCE). Attribution to 198 BCE specifically is conventional; the exact year of specific administrative reforms is not always clear.
cross_references: Part of broader Seleucid consolidation (200–192 BCE)

events.title: Han Dynasty: Emperor Wen's reign continues with administrative reforms
events.description: Emperor Wen of Han continues his reign (180–157 BCE) with a focus on administrative reform, reduction of regional kingdom autonomy, and strengthening of the central bureaucracy. In 198 BCE, the Han Dynasty is in a period of relative peace and stability, with no major military conflicts recorded. The emperor's policies are aimed at consolidating Han power and preparing for the eventual reduction of regional kingdoms' power, which will accelerate under later emperors.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, chapters on Han Dynasty
Han Shu (Book of Han) by Ban Gu

events.certainty_note: No specific event is recorded for 198 BCE in Han sources. The period is characterized by gradual administrative consolidation rather than dramatic events.
cross_references: Part of broader Han consolidation process (180–157 BCE)

events.title: Ptolemy V's regency: Egypt loses influence in the Eastern Mediterranean
events.description: Ptolemy V Epiphanes, the young Ptolemaic king, remains under regency, with real power held by court factions. Egypt is experiencing a gradual loss of influence in the Eastern Mediterranean as the Seleucid Empire under Antiochus III expands. The regency period is characterized by weak central authority and vulnerability to external pressure. Egypt's decline is a major geopolitical shift that will eventually lead to Roman intervention.
events.certainty_note: The internal instability of Egypt during Ptolemy V's regency is well-attested, though specific events in 198 BCE are not always clearly dated.
cross_references: Part of broader Ptolemaic decline (200–180 BCE)

disconfirming_evidence: The Second Macedonian War is sometimes portrayed as a straightforward Roman victory over Macedon. However, modern scholarship has emphasized the complexity of the conflict: Philip V was a capable general, and the war was not a foregone conclusion. Additionally, the war was partly motivated by Roman concerns about Macedonian expansion and the balance of power in Greece, not simply by a desire to 'liberate' Greece. The narrative of Roman liberation of Greece should be read with skepticism.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 198 BCE is dominated by Greco-Roman sources (Livy, Polybius), which privilege Mediterranean and Near Eastern events. Chinese sources (Shiji, Han Shu) provide good documentation of Han Dynasty affairs but are compiled centuries after the events. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military history; economic, social, and cultural developments are underrepresented. Sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, and the Americas are essentially absent from contemporary documentation.

graph_edges.note: Flamininus's campaigns in 198 BCE set the stage for the decisive Battle of Cynoscephalae in 197 BCE.
